ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fish curry entry; no Goan-specific variant exists. Mapped to closest available class. Rice component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish curry with rice — composite dish, approximate match under mixed dishes with fish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a Goan fish curry specific code. A0CKM (fish-based composite dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for Goan fish curry with rice identified in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Goan fish curry with steamed rice weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g steamed rice and 150 g fish curry with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Goan Fish Curry with Steamed Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values preserved as supplied. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from ICMR-NIN coconut milk fish curry reference and USDA SR Legacy fish and coconut milk components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — white rice (boiled) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Goan fish curry with steamed rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 68 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white steamed rice (GI ~68–72 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The fish curry component (coconut milk, fish, spices) contributes negligible carbohydrate and is expected to modestly lower the composite GI via fat and protein co-ingestion. GL per serving calculated as: GI (68) × carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 0.2519 = 88.2 g) / 100 = 59.9 g; however, effective available carbohydrate adjusted for fibre (88.2 − 13.0 fibre) = 75.2 g available carbohydrate; GL = 68 × 44.9 g (rice portion carbohydrate ~200 g × 0.2519 = 50.4 g available) / 100 ≈ 30.5. Conservative mid-range estimate used.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g with potassium 327 mg/100 g providing a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is well within the low-sodium threshold of 400 mg/100 g, and the high potassium content (327 mg/100 g) actively supports vascular tone; patients with hypertension may consume a standard portion but should request reduced-salt preparation and light coconut milk to minimise saturated fat and any cumulative sodium from tamarind and added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~68) driven by white rice; dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g) and fish protein attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice component carries a medium GI of approximately 68, producing a glycaemic load of ~30 per standard plate; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, increase the fish-to-rice ratio, and consider requesting brown rice substitution to reduce the glycaemic load and improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Coconut milk saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g estimated) partially offset by omega-3 fatty acids from fish and anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish (mackerel or pomfret) provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and the spice blend (turmeric, ginger) offers anti-inflammatory polyphenols; however, coconut milk contributes saturated fat estimated at ~2.1 g/100 g, so patients with cardiovascular disease should request light coconut milk, limit portion to one standard plate, and ensure the dish is not prepared with additional oil or salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from fish and curcumin/ginger anti-inflammatory polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish-based omega-3 fatty acids combined with turmeric (curcumin) and ginger provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; patients should monitor tolerance to nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) as solanine may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals, and should request reduced coconut milk to lower saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goan Fish Curry with Steamed Rice is a traditional South Indian coastal dish originating from Goa, a state on India's western coast with strong Portuguese culinary influences. The dish consists of a tangy, mildly spiced fish curry cooked in a coconut milk base with tamarind for sourness, tomatoes, onion, garlic, ginger, turmeric, red chilli, coriander, cumin, and curry leaves, served alongside plain steamed white rice. The fish used is typically mackerel (bangda) or pomfret, both of which are rich in omega-3 fatty acids. The curry sauce is characteristically orange-red in colour, moderately thick, and delivers a balance of sour, spicy, and mildly sweet flavours from the coconut milk. The texture contrasts the soft, flaky fish with the fluffy steamed rice. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notable at 3.7 g/100 g, and potassium is high at 327 mg/100 g. Sodium is moderate at 231 mg/100 g. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. It is widely available in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, particularly in Little India. Clinically, the fish omega-3 content supports cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health, while the moderate GI (~68) and fibre content make portion control important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. The dish is suitable for hypertension management given its favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.

confidence_reason: Core per_100g nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all fields. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy component data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous white rice value with documented rationale. All mandatory multilingual names provided.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish not reported in HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Goan fish curry with steamed rice composite not available in HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN databases as of 2026. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: no trans fats expected in a traditionally prepared fish curry with coconut milk and steamed rice; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy fish and coconut milk entries. cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 in HPB source; this is plausible for a composite dish where fish cholesterol is diluted by the large rice and sauce volume, accepted as reported. Saturated fat estimated at 2.1 g/100 g based on ICMR-NIN coconut milk fish curry reference and USDA SR Legacy coconut milk (NDB 12117) and mackerel (NDB 15046) component proportions; this is an approximation and should be verified against direct HPB saturated fat data when available. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white steamed rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
